q: The double-helix structure of DNA was discovered by which scientists?
options: Gregor Mendel and Hugo de Vries
explanation: Watson & Crick proposed DNA double-helix in 1953 using Franklin's X-ray data (Photo 51) and Wilkins. Nobel 1962 (Franklin had died 1958).

q: In DNA, adenine always pairs with which base?
explanation: A pairs with T (2 H-bonds) in DNA. G pairs with C (3 H-bonds). Uracil replaces Thymine in RNA. Chargaff: A=T, G=C.

q: Which nitrogenous base is present in RNA but NOT in DNA?
explanation: Uracil (U) is found only in RNA, replacing Thymine. DNA has A,T,G,C; RNA has A,U,G,C.

q: The synthesis of mRNA from a DNA template is called:
explanation: Transcription: DNA→mRNA in nucleus (RNA polymerase). Replication=DNA→DNA. Translation=mRNA→protein at ribosome.

q: Translation (protein synthesis) occurs at which organelle?
explanation: Translation occurs at ribosomes. mRNA codons read by tRNA; rRNA forms ribosome. Nucleus=transcription. Golgi=protein packaging.

q: Mendel's Law of Segregation is also known as:
explanation: Segregation = Purity of Gametes: alleles separate during gamete formation; each gamete gets one allele. Shown by monohybrid cross 3:1.

q: In a monohybrid cross Tt × Tt, the phenotypic ratio is:
explanation: Phenotypic ratio 3:1 (3 tall:1 short). Genotypic 1:2:1 (TT:Tt:tt). 1:1 is test cross (Tt×tt).

q: In a dihybrid cross RrYy × RrYy, the phenotypic ratio is:
explanation: Dihybrid 9:3:3:1 (9 round-yellow:3 round-green:3 wrinkled-yellow:1 wrinkled-green). Demonstrates Independent Assortment.

q: The genotypic ratio of a monohybrid cross (Tt × Tt) is:
explanation: Genotypic 1:2:1 (1 TT:2 Tt:1 tt). Phenotypic 3:1 (TT+Tt = tall; tt = short). Many confuse phenotype with genotype.

q: In humans, the sex of a child is determined by which parent?
options: The father (X→daughter, Y→son; mother always gives X)
explanation: Father determines sex: X sperm→daughter (XX), Y sperm→son (XY). Mother always contributes X. SRY gene on Y triggers male development.

q: Haemophilia is an example of which inheritance pattern?
options: X-linked recessive (males more affected; Royal disease)
explanation: Haemophilia = X-linked recessive (factor VIII/IX deficiency). Males hemizygous for X express it. 'Royal disease' via Queen Victoria. Sickle cell/thalassemia are autosomal.

q: Red-green colour blindness is inherited in which pattern?
options: X-linked recessive (opsin genes on X; 8% males)
explanation: Colour blindness = X-linked recessive. Males express with one affected X; females need two. Father cannot pass to sons (passes Y).

q: Down syndrome is caused by which chromosomal abnormality?
explanation: Down = Trisomy 21 (3 copies of chr 21). Most common autosomal aneuploidy. Risk ↑ with maternal age. Features: MR, flat face, simian crease, heart defects.

q: Turner syndrome has which karyotype?
options: 45,X0 (monosomy X — female, sterile, only viable monosomy)
explanation: Turner = 45,X0. Female, short, webbed neck, sterile, no puberty. Only viable human monosomy. Klinefelter=47,XXY (male).

q: Klinefelter syndrome karyotype is:
explanation: Klinefelter = 47,XXY. Male, tall, gynaecomastia, small testes, sterile, low testosterone. Most common sex chromosome aneuploidy.

q: Sickle cell anaemia is inherited in which pattern?
explanation: Sickle cell = autosomal recessive (β-globin on chr 11). HbSS=disease, HbAS=trait. Trait confers malaria resistance (balanced polymorphism).

q: The sickle cell mutation involves substitution of which amino acid?
options: Glutamic acid → Valine at position 6 of β-globin (GAG→GTG)
explanation: Point mutation: GAG(Glu)→GTG(Val) at position 6 of β-globin. HbA→HbS. Single nucleotide change. Pauling identified HbS (1949, first molecular disease).

q: Thalassemia is a genetic disorder affecting:
explanation: Thalassemia = reduced α or β globin synthesis → microcytic anaemia, transfusion-dependent. Autosomal recessive. Common in Bihar.

q: In pedigree analysis, a trait in every generation affecting both sexes equally is likely:
explanation: Autosomal dominant: every generation, both sexes equal, 50% transmission. Examples: Huntington's, achondroplasia. X-linked recessive skips generations, males more affected.

q: Which RNA carries amino acids to the ribosome during translation?
explanation: tRNA carries amino acids; anticodon pairs with mRNA codon at ribosome. mRNA=code carrier. rRNA=ribosome structure. snRNA=splicing.

q: How many chromosomes are in a normal human somatic cell?
explanation: 46 chromosomes (23 pairs): 22 autosome pairs + 1 sex pair (XX/XY). Diploid (2n). Gametes are haploid (n=23).

q: DNA replication is 'semi-conservative' because:
options: Original fully preserved, new copy made (conservative)
Each new DNA has one old + one new strand (Meselson-Stahl 1958)
Only half DNA replicated at a time
explanation: Semi-conservative: each daughter DNA = 1 parental + 1 new strand. Proven by Meselson-Stahl (1958) using 15N/14N E. coli. Universal in all life.

q: Mendel performed his landmark experiments on which organism?
explanation: Mendel used pea plant (Pisum sativum), 7 traits, ~29,000 plants, Brunn/Brno. Published 1866, rediscovered 1900. Drosophila was used by Morgan (Nobel 1933).

q: A 'test cross' is performed by crossing an organism of unknown genotype with:
options: Homozygous recessive (tt — reveals if TT or Tt)
explanation: Test cross: unknown × homozygous recessive. All dominant offspring→unknown was TT. 1:1 ratio→unknown was Tt. Recessive parent reveals unknown's gametes.

q: Makhana (Euryale ferox), genetically researched at Darbhanga (Bihar), is significant because:
options: Aquatic food crop of North Bihar; Bihar >85% of India's makhana; Mithila Makhana GI tag 2010; ICAR-RIU Darbhanga does genetic improvement
explanation: Makhana = aquatic crop of Mithila ponds/wetlands. Bihar >85% national production. GI tag 2010. ICAR-RIU Darbhanga: germplasm, polyploidy (2n=58), high-yielding varieties. Not cotton/silk/wheat.
